comment_text.sn4.23:1.1: Compare the stories of Channa (MN 144, SN 35.87), Vakkali (SN 22.87), and the suicidal Saṅgha (SN 54.9, Bu Pj 3).
comment_text.sn4.23:1.4: “Temporary” (sāmayikaṁ) liberation is mentioned at MN 122:4.1, where it contrasts with the “irreversible” liberation of the perfected one. It also appears in AN 6.44 and AN 10.75, and is defined in the Paṭisambhidāmagga as the four absorptions and four formless attainments (Ps 1.5:26.2).
comment_text.sn4.23:1.5: The commentary explains that he was chronically ill, so could not progress in meditation despite strenuous efforts over a long time.
comment_text.sn4.23:2.3: The idiomatic phrase satthahāraka (its verbal form here satthaṁ āhareyyaṁ is reflexive optative first singular) is difficult. The obvious reading is to take sattha as “knife”. However, that yields the sense “knife-bringer”, “assassin”, whereas the use of idaṁ in MN 145:5.32 shows it must be neuter, i.e. it is a thing not a person. Richard Gombrich suggests we read sattha here as equivalent to Sanskrit śvasita, “breathing, life” (see Brahmali’s note on Bu Pj 3:2.49.1), yielding the sense “life-taker”, “breath-stealer”, “deadly weapon”. This agrees with the Vinaya commentary, which explains, “What does it take? Life.” (Kiṃ harati? Jīvitaṁ). Since this idiom is only used in the context of suicide, I use the normal English term.
comment_text.sn4.23:3.1: Māra uncharacteristically showers the Buddha with praise. It seems he is afraid that Godhika will slip beyond his grasp.
comment_text.sn4.23:4.4: “Light-bringer” (jutindhara) is a curious epithet for Māra to use, not least because it is etymologically parallel to “Lucifer”.
comment_text.sn4.23:7.4: The Buddha confirms that he had attained arahantship, or irreversible liberation, which must have been shortly before he died. The Buddhist tradition says that only an arahant is allowed to take their own life, and only then in cases of terminal illness. However, it is possible to read the scriptural passages the other way. Since an arahant is incapable of doing anything wrong, the fact that they killed themselves shows there is nothing wrong with voluntary euthanasia in cases of incurable and imminent terminal illness.
comment_text.sn4.23:9.2: Vivatta (or vivaṭṭa) often means “cast off”, as in “the fetters” (vivattayi saṁyojanaṁ) or the “veil” (vivaṭṭacchada). Here it describes the dead monk’s body after attaining final Nibbana.
comment_text.sn4.23:10.7: For the “arched harp” (vīṇa), see SN 35.246 and notes. Pañcasikha’s harp is described in the same way (DN 21:1.2.4), and tradition says they were in fact the same instrument.
comment_text.sn4.23:14.1: This verse, which must have been added by redactors, also occurs at Snp 3.2:25.1.
comment_text.sn4.23:14.3: Māra is referred to as a yakkha.

root_text.sn4.23:1.2: ekaṁ samayaṁ bhagavā rājagahe viharati veḷuvane kalandakanivāpe.
root_text.sn4.23:1.3: Tena kho pana samayena āyasmā godhiko isigilipasse viharati kāḷasilāyaṁ.
root_text.sn4.23:1.4: Atha kho āyasmā godhiko appamatto ātāpī pahitatto viharanto sāmayikaṁ cetovimuttiṁ phusi.
root_text.sn4.23:1.5: Atha kho āyasmā godhiko tamhā sāmayikāya cetovimuttiyā parihāyi.
root_text.sn4.23:1.6: Dutiyampi kho āyasmā godhiko appamatto ātāpī pahitatto viharanto sāmayikaṁ cetovimuttiṁ phusi.
root_text.sn4.23:1.7: Dutiyampi kho āyasmā godhiko tamhā sāmayikāya cetovimuttiyā parihāyi.
root_text.sn4.23:1.8: Tatiyampi kho āyasmā godhiko appamatto ātāpī pahitatto viharanto sāmayikaṁ cetovimuttiṁ phusi.
root_text.sn4.23:1.9: Tatiyampi kho āyasmā godhiko tamhā …pe… parihāyi.
root_text.sn4.23:1.10: Catutthampi kho āyasmā godhiko appamatto …pe… vimuttiṁ phusi.
root_text.sn4.23:1.11: Catutthampi kho āyasmā godhiko tamhā …pe… parihāyi.
root_text.sn4.23:1.12: Pañcamampi kho āyasmā godhiko …pe… cetovimuttiṁ phusi.
root_text.sn4.23:1.14: Chaṭṭhampi kho āyasmā godhiko appamatto ātāpī pahitatto viharanto sāmayikaṁ cetovimuttiṁ phusi.
root_text.sn4.23:1.15: Chaṭṭhampi kho āyasmā godhiko tamhā sāmayikāya cetovimuttiyā parihāyi.
root_text.sn4.23:1.16: Sattamampi kho āyasmā godhiko appamatto ātāpī pahitatto viharanto sāmayikaṁ cetovimuttiṁ phusi.
root_text.sn4.23:2.4: Atha kho māro pāpimā āyasmato godhikassa cetasā cetoparivitakkamaññāya yena bhagavā tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā bhagavantaṁ gāthāhi ajjhabhāsi:
root_text.sn4.23:6.1: Tena kho pana samayena āyasmatā godhikena satthaṁ āharitaṁ hoti.
root_text.sn4.23:6.2: Atha kho bhagavā “māro ayaṁ pāpimā” iti viditvā māraṁ pāpimantaṁ gāthāya ajjhabhāsi:
root_text.sn4.23:8.2: “āyāma, bhikkhave, yena isigilipassaṁ kāḷasilā tenupasaṅkamissāma yattha godhikena kulaputtena satthaṁ āharitan”ti.
root_text.sn4.23:8.3: “Evaṁ, bhante”ti kho te bhikkhū bhagavato paccassosuṁ.
root_text.sn4.23:9.1: Atha kho bhagavā sambahulehi bhikkhūhi saddhiṁ yena isigilipassaṁ kāḷasilā tenupasaṅkami.
root_text.sn4.23:9.2: Addasā kho bhagavā āyasmantaṁ godhikaṁ dūratova mañcake vivattakkhandhaṁ semānaṁ.
root_text.sn4.23:9.3: Tena kho pana samayena dhūmāyitattaṁ timirāyitattaṁ gacchateva purimaṁ disaṁ, gacchati pacchimaṁ disaṁ, gacchati uttaraṁ disaṁ, gacchati dakkhiṇaṁ disaṁ, gacchati uddhaṁ, gacchati adho, gacchati anudisaṁ.
root_text.sn4.23:10.2: “passatha no tumhe, bhikkhave, etaṁ dhūmāyitattaṁ timirāyitattaṁ gacchateva purimaṁ disaṁ, gacchati pacchimaṁ disaṁ, gacchati uttaraṁ disaṁ, gacchati dakkhiṇaṁ disaṁ, gacchati uddhaṁ, gacchati adho, gacchati anudisan”ti?
root_text.sn4.23:10.4: “Eso kho, bhikkhave, māro pāpimā godhikassa kulaputtassa viññāṇaṁ samanvesati:
root_text.sn4.23:10.6: Appatiṭṭhitena ca, bhikkhave, viññāṇena godhiko kulaputto parinibbuto”ti.
root_text.sn4.23:10.7: Atha kho māro pāpimā beluvapaṇḍuvīṇaṁ ādāya yena bhagavā tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā bhagavantaṁ gāthāya ajjhabhāsi:

translation_text.sn4.23:1.2: At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha, in the Bamboo Grove, the squirrels’ feeding ground.
translation_text.sn4.23:1.3: Now at that time Venerable Godhika was staying on the slopes of Isigili at the Black Rock.
translation_text.sn4.23:1.4: Then Venerable Godhika, meditating diligent, keen, and resolute, experienced temporary freedom of heart.
translation_text.sn4.23:1.5: But then he fell away from that temporary freedom of heart.
translation_text.sn4.23:1.14: sixth time Godhika experienced temporary freedom of heart.
translation_text.sn4.23:1.15: But for a sixth time he fell away from it.
translation_text.sn4.23:1.16: For a seventh time Godhika, meditating diligent, keen, and resolute, experienced temporary freedom of heart.
translation_text.sn4.23:2.2: “I’ve fallen away from this temporary freedom of heart no less than six times.
translation_text.sn4.23:2.4: And then Māra the Wicked, knowing Godhika’s train of thought, went up to the Buddha and addressed him in verse:
translation_text.sn4.23:3.3: You’ve gone beyond all threats and perils,
translation_text.sn4.23:3.4: I bow to your feet, O Clear-eyed One!
translation_text.sn4.23:5.1: For Blessed One, renowned among men,
translation_text.sn4.23:5.2: your disciple who loves your teaching,
translation_text.sn4.23:5.3: a trainee who hasn’t achieved their heart’s desire—
translation_text.sn4.23:6.1: Now at that time Venerable Godhika had already committed suicide.
translation_text.sn4.23:6.2: Then the Buddha, knowing that this was Māra the Wicked, addressed him in verse:
translation_text.sn4.23:7.2: for they don’t long for life.
translation_text.sn4.23:7.3: Having plucked out craving, root and all,
translation_text.sn4.23:8.1: Then the Buddha said to the mendicants,
translation_text.sn4.23:8.2: “Come, mendicants, let’s go to the Black Rock on the slopes of Isigili where Godhika, who came from a good family, committed suicide.”
translation_text.sn4.23:9.1: Then the Buddha together with several mendicants went to the Black Rock on the slopes of Isigili.
translation_text.sn4.23:9.2: The Buddha saw Godhika off in the distance on his cot, lying with aggregates cast off.
translation_text.sn4.23:9.3: Now at that time a cloud of black smoke was moving east, west, north, south, above, below, and in-between.
translation_text.sn4.23:10.1: Then the Buddha said to the mendicants,
translation_text.sn4.23:10.2: “Mendicants, do you see that cloud of black smoke moving east, west, north, south, above, below, and in-between?”
translation_text.sn4.23:10.4: “That’s Māra the Wicked searching for Godhika’s consciousness, wondering:
translation_text.sn4.23:10.6: But since his consciousness is not established, Godhika is quenched.”
translation_text.sn4.23:10.7: Then Māra, carrying his arched harp made from the pale timber of wood-apple, went up to the Buddha and addressed him in verse:
translation_text.sn4.23:11.2: in the four quarters and in-between,
translation_text.sn4.23:11.4: where has that Godhika got to?”
translation_text.sn4.23:12.1: “He was an attentive and steadfast sage,
translation_text.sn4.23:12.3: By day and by night he applied himself,
translation_text.sn4.23:13.1: He defeated the army of death,
translation_text.sn4.23:13.2: and won’t return for any future life.
translation_text.sn4.23:13.3: Having plucked out craving, root and all
translation_text.sn4.23:14.2: that his harp dropped from his armpit,

variant_text.sn4.23:14.4: tatthevantaradhāyathāti → tatthevantaradhāyithāti (sya-all, km); tatthevantaradhayathāti (pts1ed); tattheva antaradhāyīti (mr)
